mnemonics.title: Array of Structures Declaration
mnemonics.content: REMEMBER: TYPE NAME[SIZE]; The structure type comes first, then array name with size in square brackets. Example: struct Student class[50];

mnemonics.title: Accessing Members
mnemonics.content: INDEX BEFORE DOT: arr[i].member - always use index first, then dot operator. Think: HOUSE first, then ROOM.

mnemonics.title: Memory Calculation
mnemonics.content: TOTAL MEMORY = STRUCT SIZE × ARRAY SIZE. Find struct size first (account for padding), then multiply by number of elements.

acronyms.usage: Refers to a collection of structure variables stored in contiguous memory, used for managing multiple records like students, employees
Using malloc()/free() to allocate array of structures at runtime when size is not known at compile time

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a table where each ROW is one structure element, and each COLUMN is a member. Array of structures is like multiple rows of the same record type stored one after another contiguously in memory.
Think of array name as street address, index as house number, and member as specific room inside the house. students[3].name means: go to street (students), house #3, enter room (name).
When you add 1 to a structure pointer, it jumps by the entire size of one structure, not by 1 byte. Visualize stepping from one row to the next in a table.

analogies.analogy: Like a classroom seating chart where each seat (array element) contains a complete student record with name, roll number, marks - all information grouped together for one student, repeated for all students.
Think of houses in a society built side by side - each house (structure) has multiple rooms (members), and all houses occupy consecutive plots (memory locations).
Writing struct Student class[50] is like saying '50 students of type Student' - the type first, then quantity, just like '50 chairs' or '30 books'.
